extra.reasoning:
Property: G(opted_out implies not sent). I checked which transitions can set sent=true. t2 sets sent=true only when not opted_out (safe). The only transition setting sent=true while opted_out is true is t4, which requires (opted_out and acc==21). optout (t1) and t3 always set sent=false; the step transitions also set sent=false. So a violation requires reaching a state with opted_out=true and acc==21, then firing send_attempt.

acc only changes via step events. Each step computes acc := (3*acc + c) mod 64 (the triple-nested conditional subtraction of 64 implements mod 64, since max 3*63+63=252 needs at most 3 subtractions): step_0 c=35, step_1 c=31, step_2 c=63. Starting from acc=0, I computed the closure of {0} under these three functions: it is exactly {0,3,4,7,8,11,12,15,16,19,20,23,24,27,28,31,32,35,36,39,40,43,44,47,48,51,52,55,56,59,60,63} (32 values, all ≡ 0 or 3 mod 4). 21 ≡ 1 mod 4, so acc==21 is never reachable. Hence t4's guard is never satisfiable and sent is never set true while opted_out is true.

I also performed a full BFS over the complete reachable state space (opted_out, sent, acc) under all five events: 96 reachable states, zero satisfying (opted_out and sent). Since the entire reachable state space is finite and fully explored, no execution of any length (well within the 66-event bound) can violate the property. The property holds.
